posts.text:
OP clarified elsewhere that they used this dataset: huggingface.co/datasets/The...

My understanding is they subsetted on 1837–1899, which per the dataset card is ~13M pp (so ~464 pp/doc). Call it ~500 tokens/page, that's about ~65M tokens. 

Still small, but "28k docs" doesn't tell the full story.

reply_context.text: you can't build a functioning chatbot from a corpus of only 28,000 pieces of anything. that's not enough training data to get the most basic "conversation" out of it.

posts.text: When I think of "incoherent" LLM generations, what comes to mind is the kind of content I've seen generated by checkpoints that were undertrained or overcooked. Calling it schizophrenic nonsense would be generous. Violation of basic grammar, heavy repetition of words/phrases... could be way worse.

reply_context.text: When you're trying to judge an LLM for "coherence", the word I usual have in mind is "plausibility". If you handed me those outputs in the absence of the prompts, I wouldn't immediately be like "this is LLM generated garbage". The content is at least sufficiently coherent to be plausible.

posts.text: When you're trying to judge an LLM for "coherence", the word I usual have in mind is "plausibility". It's almost unfair to attempt long term climate modeling without also including high resolution political and economic modeling. Everything is connected.

reply_context.text: I think your community is also hamstrung by under-specified models. The problem space is hard enough already, but there's no way climate models could account for things like a significant fraction of the world's oil depots being lit on fire, which surely will have both near and long term impacts.

posts.text: I think your community is also hamstrung by under-specified models. The problem space is hard enough already, but there's no way climate models could account for things like a significant fraction of the world's oil depots being lit on fire, which surely will have both near and long term impacts.

reply_context.text: Definitely. I think the bigger issue here is that we have *nether* at present when it comes to upper tail extremes and historically unseen "rare/weird stuff." We're at the point where we really, truly need to know where we stand in that regard with latest generation models, and we don't.

Standardized protocols != standardized datasets. Strong recommend that the meteorological community emphasize the former over the latter. 

Look at the LLM evaluation space as a warning to how benchmarks lose value over time. 

Relevant theory explaining why this happens: dl.acm.org/doi/abs/10.1...

This underscores the need for consensus-driven, standardized evaluation protocols."

posts.text: Moreover, the way this model seems biased to avoid discussing the existence of support for slavery (which would have been contemporaneous with the time range of the training data) has me suspecting it was built on top of a base model that had already been safety-tuned.

reply_context.text: I'm a bit sus about this "entirely from scratch" claim. 28k documents is an extremely small corpus to train a language model, especially one as coherent as this. If the average document length was 100pp, this corpus would be just barely larger than TinyStories.

posts.text: I'm confident we'll discover new, more effective ways to promote authenticity. This is just an annoying phase of a cycle we've been through before multiple times. It would be easier if the levers of power weren't being held by fraudsters, but they can only slow progress---not stop it.

posts.text: That means the feed needs to intelligently choose content that can serve as a sort of "ambassador" for collections of content that might otherwise overwhelm my feed.

reply_context.text: In addition to "phasing" the rhythm of the feed to respect my affect (positive oriented content when I start my day, then present me with news after I'm in the mindset to process it), I want a feed that is appropriately "paced" as well.

posts.text: In addition to "phasing" the rhythm of the feed to respect my affect (positive oriented content when I start my day, then present me with news after I'm in the mindset to process it), I want a feed that is appropriately "paced" as well.

reply_context.text: If I know about an event, I can dig up details on it. But if something happened earlier in the day and by the afternoon everyone is talking about something else, the earlier thing might not even cross my radar. My feed gets overwhelmed by all of the immediate discourse.
